summary: This satirical piece presents fake tech news stories that skewer various targets in the software industry. Yegge lampoons Java's verbosity and Eclipse's notorious startup time, takes shots at Ruby on Rails' scaling problems, mocks Microsoft's legacy code, and delivers a pointed commentary on the absurdity of using recursion questions in technical interviews. The piece is structured as a mock newsletter with increasingly absurd stories, culminating in juvenile humor about a vacuum cleaner.
key_points: Java's design pattern culture creates meaningless complexity - combining pattern names produces absurd constructs like 'FactoryObserverBridgeChainOfCommandSingletonProxy'
Eclipse IDE was notorious for extremely slow startup times and unresponsiveness
Ruby on Rails faced legitimate scaling criticism in 2007, satirized here with URLs that could delete databases
The recursion vs iteration equivalence is humorously 'disproven' by noting recursion's real purpose: filtering interview candidates
Microsoft's legacy codebase issues are mocked by suggesting Vista still runs code from Bill Gates' childhood
money_quotes: Java programmers understand an average of seven fewer Computer Science concepts per hour spent with Java each day
The last thing anyone heard him say aloud was the cryptic comment: 'I see the splash screen is appropriately blue.'
The little security system that Bill wrote at age 12 and hacked into a year later is still in production today, in Windows Vista
Some of our users are just plain wankers
There is in fact one thing you can do using recursion that you can't do using iteration, namely: 'You can use it to weed out stupid fugging interview candidates.'
key_insight: Tech industry sacred cows - from Java's enterprise patterns to algorithm-focused interviews - deserve relentless mockery because they've become rituals disconnected from actual software quality.
